Uh, then obviously we're available for committee questions. A CC continues to support injured New Zealanders. It's been a treasured part of New Zealand society for over 50 years, delivering no fault insurance for personal injury. We know New Zealanders value the scheme, and we are very aware of our role as stewards of the scheme to make sure it's available for current and future generations.

For the last decade, the size of the scheme has continued to grow each year in volume of claims and in cost to support and rehabilitate injured people. In 2025, A CC received more than 2 million claims, 71,000 more than the year before. We accepted 99% of these claims.

Of the new claims, 55% were accepted within a minute, and 80% were accepted within 24 hours. More complicated claims such as treatment injuries, sensitive claims, and work-related Mental injuries take longer to assess, and on average, it took 57 days to assure a cover decision on these cohort, we spent over 8.1 billion in medical treatment, rehabilitation support, and weekly compensation, an increase of 1 billion over the previous year.

The overall deficit of the scheme for the year was 1.5 billion, significantly better than the 2024 deficit of 7.2 billion. This was primarily due to the reduction in the outstanding claims liability, which is the future cost of known claims. A CC managers one of New Zealand's biggest investment funds of 54 billion, and the investment team performed very well with returns above the benchmark.

Again, the investment funders decide to ensure there are sufficient reserves to cover the lifetime cost of injuries that have already occurred. This reflects the principle of intergenerational equity. However, the total account reserves with 13.8 billion negative as asset during 2025.

Accs experienced a year of internal and external challenges. The scheme is under pressure with more people spending more time away from work after injury, taking more time to recover while the cost paid for treatment and rehabilitation continues to rise. The two, two key drivers of the increase in outstanding claims liability are the volume and costs claims and the rehabilitation performance of returning people to work or independence.

Claims costs grew with inflation and wage growth for health services, and the gap between our funding for levies and appropriations and the new year claims costs stubbornly remains deterioration and rehabilitation performance is an ongoing challenge.

Continuing the decade long trend, despite the hard work about people and the providers. Accs injury prevention activities have also not yet made a significant impact on the reduction of injuries, but we are taking action to tackle the challenges.

We are determined to be a high performing organization with a safe, positive, and inclusive culture, focusing on our core purpose of supporting people to recover from their injuries. There have been three reviews conducted over the past 12 months. The board commissioned an external review of accs culture.

The minister for a CC commissioned a review of accs case management practices, and Treasury commissioned a review of accs investment function. The board and executive welcomed the guidance provided by the reviews. We've committed to acting on the recommendations from each of them, and work has commenced.

A CC has developed a turnaround plan, which includes the recommendations that is aimed at improving social rehabilitation decision making, supporting clients to return to work or independence, working differently with providers and resetting a C C's strategic direction priorities.

We recognize we can improve our performance and have started work on initiatives and claims management, our operational decision making, how we engage with providers and our cultural reset, a new strategy to strengthen our core and direct our efforts will be shared with our people this month.

We are pleased that there have been promising signs in our rehabilitation performance as a result of the changes in our case management practices. The return to work and independence rates are improving, and the growth rate in the long term claims pool has reduced expenditure across key areas of weekly compensation.

Social rehabilitation and conflict of surgery is forecast to be lower than our budget, reflecting performance improvements and our deliberate focus on the management of costs. I want to thank our frontline staff in particular for the huge effort to achieve these performance results.

But while we recognize our part in ensuring the scheme is sustainable, we need others to consider the impact they're having on the scheme's future given over the next 12 months. for future claimants. The costs of a CC are paid by everyone, and we all have a part to play in protecting it for future generations. I'll now hand over to Megan for her comments. Thanks, Jan.
